data.title: NextEra targets high end of $3.92-$4.02 2026 adjusted EPS range as FPL outlines $90B-$100B 2032 investment plan
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NextEra Energy (NEE) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "NextEra Energy is off to a terrific start to the year, delivering strong first quarter results" and "Adjusted earnings per share increased by 10% year-over-year" (President, CEO & Chairman John Ketchum).

  • "FPL expects to invest between $90 billion and $100 billion through 2032" and its 10-year plan "shows roughly 4 gigawatts of new gas-fired generation, complementing over 12 gigawatts of solar and over 7 gigawatts of storage solutions" (President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum).

  • "So far, we have about 21 gigawatts of large load interest at FPL" and "we continue to expect at least one large load customer to sign up for capacity under FPL's tariff by the end of the year"; "every gigawatt of large load under FPL's approved tariff" was described as "roughly $2 billion of CapEx" (President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum).

  • "Energy Resources" had "a record quarter" with "4 gigawatts of new long-term contracted renewables and storage projects" added to backlog, including "1.3 gigawatts" of battery storage origination; management also cited a stand-alone and co-located storage pipeline of "over 110 gigawatts" (President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum).

  • "Last month, the U.S. Department of Commerce selected Energy Resources to build 9.5 gigawatts of new gas-fired generation to serve large load" tied to the "U.S.-Japan trade deal" framework; "the U.S. and Japan would own the projects, while Energy Resources would develop, build and operate them" (President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum).

  • "For the first quarter of 2026, FPL's earnings per share increased $0.06 year-over-year" and "FPL's capital expenditures were approximately $3.2 billion for the quarter" (CFO & Executive VP of Finance Michael Dunne).

Outlook

  • "Our 2026 adjusted earnings per share expectations range of $3.92 to $4.02 remains unchanged, and we are targeting the high end of that range" (CFO Dunne).

  • "We expect to grow adjusted earnings per share at a compound annual growth rate of 8% plus through 2032" and "are targeting the same from 2032 through 2035, all off the 2025 base of $3.71 adjusted earnings per share" (CFO Dunne).

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management again said "our 2026 adjusted earnings per share expectation ranges of $3.92 to $4.02 per share remain unchanged" and "we are targeting the high end of that range" (CFO Dunne, Q4 2025).

Financial Results

  • "Adjusted earnings per share increased by 10% year-over-year" in Q1, with performance attributed to "both FPL and Energy Resources" (President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum).

  • "Energy Resources" reported "adjusted earnings growth of approximately 14% year-over-year" while "the comparative contribution from a customer supply business decreased by $0.04 per share" (CFO Dunne).

  • "FPL's first quarter retail sales increased by approximately 3.4% year-over-year" and, "after taking weather into account," increased "roughly 0.3%" on a weather-normalized basis (CFO Dunne).

  • "Our backlog now totals approximately 33 gigawatts" after accounting for "0.3 gigawatts of new projects placed into service since our last earnings call" (CFO Dunne).

Q&A

  • Steven Fleishman, Wolfe Research: Asked for milestones, turbine supply, and pipeline/transmission access on the U.S.-Japan projects; President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum said definitive agreements were expected "in the next 2- to 3-month period," added "we'll have ample supply of the turbines," and said pipeline and transmission access were part of the development work.

  • Steven Fleishman, Wolfe Research: Asked about recontracting price changes; CFO Dunne said, "The pricing on the new contracts is roughly a $20 per megawatt hour on average increase relative to the prior realized pricing."

  • Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies: Asked whether linear infrastructure expansion could be acquisitive; President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum said, "In terms of acquisitions, sure," while adding, "[ We're always ] lean towards greenfield" and also highlighted partnering with incumbents.

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: Pressed on large-scale nuclear/consortium participation and Turkey Point; President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum said, "We would not be interested in doing that together with the consortium" and emphasized structures that "protects our customers and protects our shareholders," with focus "more around the Gen 3 technology" for SMRs.

  • William Appicelli, UBS: Asked whether backlog acceleration was tied to tax credit roll-offs; CEO & President Brian Bolster said, "we haven't actually stepped into the acceleration yet" and called it "fundamental demand" rather than pull-forward activity.

  • Nicholas Campanella, Barclays: Questioned FPL large-load assumptions and higher 2026 CapEx; CFO Dunne said, "we have not said 1 gigawatt" for large load, and linked higher CapEx to proactively "bringing that in today at locked-in prices to remove any trade impacts."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly positive, with praise such as "Nicely done, genuinely" (Julien Dumoulin-Smith, Jefferies) alongside detailed diligence on execution and returns, including questions on Japan-project timelines, FPL CapEx drivers, and nuclear contracting.

  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and mostly steady in Q&A, using definitive language including "we'll have ample supply" and reiterating strategic positioning: "We intentionally went out and shifted our strategy last year to bring your own generation" (President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum).

  • Versus Q4 2025, the call leaned more toward near-term project execution (DOC/Japan milestones and contracting cadence) than competitive threats; in Q4, President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum said of hyperscaler developer acquisitions, "the short answer is, it has no impact on our partnership" and "I couldn't be less concerned."

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance language remained consistent: Q1 reiterated the unchanged "$3.92 to $4.02" 2026 adjusted EPS range and "targeting the high end" (CFO Dunne), matching Q4.

  • Large-load messaging at FPL showed broader funnel metrics: Q1 cited "about 21 gigawatts" of interest and "about 12 gigawatts" in advanced discussions (President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum) versus Q4 citing "over 20 gigawatts" of interest and "about 9 gigawatts" in advanced discussions (President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum, Q4 2025).

  • Strategic emphasis expanded from Q4’s BYOG framing to Q1’s concrete federal selection: "selected Energy Resources to build 9.5 gigawatts of new gas-fired generation" (President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum).

Risks and Concerns

  • Execution and contracting risk on the U.S.-Japan projects was framed around completing definitive agreements; President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum said the company was working to complete agreements "in the next 2- to 3-month period" with "milestones with payments tied to those milestones."

  • Gas build-out constraints were tied to labor and permitting: President, CEO & Chairman Ketchum said the "biggest constraint" was "labor" and added, "We have got to get permitting reform done in this country."

  • Interest-rate and trade impacts were addressed via hedging and supply positioning; CFO Dunne said the company had an "over $43 billion interest rate hedging program" and had secured key components (including solar, batteries, wind components, and transformer capacity) to support build plans.

Final Takeaway

NextEra management emphasized accelerating U.S. power demand, pointed to a record 4-gigawatt quarter of contracted renewables and storage at Energy Resources, and highlighted a new federal selection tied to a 9.5-gigawatt gas generation build where the company would develop, build, and operate while the U.S. and Japan would own the assets. The company reiterated its unchanged 2026 adjusted EPS range of $3.92 to $4.02 while targeting the high end, framed FPL’s multiyear capital plan at $90 billion to $100 billion through 2032, and positioned large-load growth as near-term, stating it expects at least one large-load customer to sign under the tariff by year-end.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Independent Bank targets 4.5%-5.5% 2026 loan growth while merger cost saves phase in
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Independent Bank Corporation (IBCP) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Independent Bank Corporation reported first quarter 2026 net income of $16.9 million or $0.81 per diluted share versus net income of $15.6 million or $0.74 per diluted share in the prior year period.” (President, CEO & Director William Kessel)

  • “Highlights for our first quarter include a net interest margin of 3.65%, which is a 3 basis point increase on a linked-quarter basis; an increase in net interest income of $500,000 or 1.1% over the fourth quarter of 2025; an increase in tangible common equity per share of common stock at $0.33 or 5.9% annualized from December 31, 2025; a return on average assets and return on average equity of 1.24% and 13.43%, respectively; net growth in total deposits was brokered time deposits of $80.4 million or 6.9% annualized from December 31, 2025; net growth in loans of $31.8 million or 3% annualized from December 31, 2025; an increase in tangible common equity ratio to 8.7%.” (President, CEO & Director Kessel)

  • “We remain encouraged by our momentum and are optimistic about our opportunities and confident in the benefits of our recently announced merger with HCB Financial Corp., which will provide enhanced shareholder value.” (President, CEO & Director Kessel)

  • “Looking ahead, based on a strong pipeline, we believe we will continue low double-digit growth of our commercial loan portfolio in 2026.” (Executive VP of Commercial Lending & Chief Lending Officer Joel Rahn)

  • “Noninterest expense was $38.3 million in the first quarter, above our forecasted range of $36 million to $37 million.” (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Gavin Mohr)

Outlook

  • “Our outlook estimated full year loan growth of 4.5% to 5.5%.” (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Mohr)

  • “First quarter 2026 net interest income increased 7.3% over 2025, which is within our forecasted range of 7% to 8%.” (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Mohr)

  • “Noninterest income totaled $12 million in the first quarter of 2026, which was within our forecasted range of $11.3 million to $12.3 million in the first quarter.” (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Mohr)

  • “Not measurably, Brendan. That forecast holds.” (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Mohr), responding on whether fewer-than-embedded rate cuts would change the 2026 margin outlook.

  • Relative to the prior quarter’s setup, management’s on-call framing shifted from a rate-cut-dependent model to a steadier message that the margin outlook would not change materially even if rate cuts do not occur. (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Mohr)

Financial results

  • $0.81 diluted EPS vs. $0.79 analyst estimate for Q1 2026. (President, CEO & Director Kessel; analystsEstimates)

  • “Deposits totaled $4.9 billion at March 31, 2026, an increase of $80.4 million from year-end.” (President, CEO & Director Kessel)

  • “Our total cost of funds decreased by 13 basis points to 1.54%.” (President, CEO & Director Kessel)

  • “Total nonperforming loans were $27.5 million or 64 basis points of total loans at quarter end, up slightly from 54 basis points at 12/31.” (Executive VP of Commercial Lending & Chief Lending Officer Rahn)

  • “Noninterest income totaled $12 million in the first quarter of 2026 compared to $10.4 million in the year ago quarter and $12 million in the fourth quarter of 2025.” (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Mohr)

  • “Litigation expense was $1.5 million in the quarter attributed to an accrual established for losses we consider probable as a result of all of our outstanding litigation matters in aggregate.” (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Mohr)

Q&A

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division: asked if fewer rate cuts would change the 2026 margin calculus; CFO Mohr: “Not measurably, Brendan. That forecast holds.”

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division: asked about core funding competition in Michigan; President, CEO & Director Kessel: “I think it continues to be very competitive. In the Michigan markets, we've got a heavy field of credit unions.”

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division: asked whether geopolitical change altered the 2026 outlook; President, CEO & Director Kessel: “we continue to be very optimistic about how we expect 2026 to unfold.” Executive VP Rahn: “the high energy prices could be a drag on the economy… you could see loan growth muted, I suppose, but we've not seen that yet.”

  • Adam Kroll, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: asked if the $36 million to $37 million expense run-rate (excluding deal/nonrecurring) still holds and about deal cost saves; CFO Mohr: “No, we feel good about that, excluding the deal and the nonrecurring.” CFO Mohr: “it was announced 50% phased in, in year 1 and fully phased in, in year 2. And just to point out, that's 50% half a year.”

  • Adam Kroll, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: asked about deploying excess liquidity from the HCB deal; CFO Mohr: “we're not ready to give direction specifically on that, Adam.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on sensitivity to rate outcomes, expense normalization, and merger-related balance sheet decisions; Nosal pressed on “if we don't get any rate cuts,” and Kroll asked whether core expenses could “trend higher.” (Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division; Adam Kroll, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division)

  • Slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, with continued emphasis on “strength of our core fundamentals” and merger benefits; in Q&A, management stayed measured on macro and avoided committing on liquidity deployment, stating, “we're not ready to give direction specifically on that.” (President, CEO & Director William Kessel; CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Gavin Mohr)

  • Versus the prior quarter, management sounded similarly confident on commercial opportunities, while Q1 added more explicit macro/geopolitical caveats and more visible cost items (litigation accrual and retroactive incentives) discussed in the expense bridge. (President, CEO & Director Kessel; Executive VP Rahn; CFO Mohr)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Profitability metrics moderated from Q4 2025 levels (Q4 ROAA 1.35% and ROAE 14.75%) to Q1 2026 (ROAA 1.24% and ROAE 13.43%), while net interest margin moved from 3.62% in Q4 2025 to 3.65% in Q1 2026. (President, CEO & Director Kessel)

  • The loan-growth message stayed focused on commercial-led expansion, but Q1 explicitly acknowledged the company’s overall loan growth pace was “below our forecasted range,” while reiterating “low double-digit growth” expectations for the commercial portfolio. (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Mohr; Executive VP Rahn)

  • Analyst attention narrowed from Q4’s broader capital/M&A and securities-runoff discussion to Q1’s emphasis on margin sensitivity, expense normalization, and the integration/lending deployment implications of the HCB transaction. (Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group, LLC, Research Division; Adam Kroll, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division)

Risks and concerns

  • “Total nonperforming loans were $27.5 million… It's worth noting that $20 million of this total is one commercial development exposure that we discussed in previous quarters.” (Executive VP of Commercial Lending & Chief Lending Officer Rahn)

  • “Litigation expense was $1.5 million in the quarter” and “Nonrecurring noninterest expense items totaled approximately $1.9 million in the first quarter of 2026.” (CFO, Executive VP, Treasurer & Corporate Secretary Mohr)

  • “the high energy prices could be a drag on the economy… you could see loan growth muted” if conditions persist, while management emphasized monitoring and willingness to “react accordingly.” (Executive VP of Commercial Lending & Chief Lending Officer Rahn)

Final takeaway

Management emphasized steady margin and net interest income momentum, commercial-loan-led growth driven by banker hires, and confidence in the recently announced HCB Financial merger, while acknowledging competitive deposit pricing in Michigan and a more uncertain geopolitical backdrop. The quarter also highlighted expense noise from a litigation accrual and retroactive deposit incentives, alongside management’s message that the core expense run-rate guidance still holds when excluding deal and nonrecurring items, and that the bank is not yet ready to provide specific direction on deploying post-merger excess liquidity.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exxon, QatarEnergy ship first LNG cargo from Golden Pass export terminal
data.description:
Golden Pass LNG, the ​joint venture between Exxon Mobil (XOM) and QatarEnergy, said Wednesday it loaded and shipped its first export cargo of liquefied natural gas from its Sabine Pass terminal in Texas, bringing one of the biggest new U.S. export projects closer to full commercial service.

The company said Train 1 is now operating, while construction and commissioning continue on Trains 2 and 3, which are expected to come online in turn upon stable operation of Train 1; once fully operational, Golden Pass LNG expects to export ~18M tons/year of LNG.

The destination of the first shipment on the vessel, Al Qa'iyyahal, was unclear, but Reuters previously reported it was expected to go to Italy to ​make up for some contracted LNG QatarEnergy has ​not been able to deliver because of the Middle East war.

The inaugural ​shipment comes ​seven years ⁠after construction on the $10B plant began; QatarEnergy owns 70% of the project, and Exxon Mobil (XOM) holds 30%.

Golden Pass LNG is crucial for QatarEnergy's growth ambitions amid an uncertain outlook caused by the war, as the operator's entire 77M metric tons/year LNG production capacity in Qatar is currently offline.

data.title: Market leadership is screaming speculation
data.description:
Speculative corners of the market have taken the lead in April, with high-risk baskets posting outsized gains as investors appear to be chasing beta over fundamentals.

Goldman Sachs basket data compiled by Charles Schwab show the Quantum Index up 35% quarter-to-date as of Wednesday, the strongest performer on the screen. The Meme Stocks basket has climbed 27.9%, Non-Profitable Tech has gained 25.6% and the Most Short Rolling basket is up 20.3%. Retail Favorites has added 19.9%.

The leadership reflects a market driven by short squeezes, thematic speculation and high-volatility growth rather than defensive positioning or earnings visibility.

High Beta Cyclicals, Macro Momentum Short and US Drones have also posted strong returns during the period. 

Even the Quantum basket, still down 9.3% year to date, has surged 35% this month alone, underscoring the violent rebound in beaten-down pockets of the tape.

data.title: U.S. SEC, Treasury seek more data on private credit risks - report
data.description:
U.S. financial regulators have increased their efforts to gather information on how much risk has accumulated in the $3T private credit industry, according to a media report. While the watchdogs aren't sounding dire alarms, investor fears have motivated some backers to pull their capital from the sector.

The Securities and Exchange Commission has opened several enforcement probes of large private credit managers, the Wall Street Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter. Additionally, the U.S. Treasury Department has asked for information from private fund managers and insurance companies about their business models. The Federal Reserve is also looking into banks' lending and exposure to private credit, the report said.

Regulators have been monitoring risk from the burgeoning private credit market for years, but the scrutiny has been intensifying as investors' requests to exit the space have exceeded the standard limits that most funds place on redemptions. Earlier this month, the Financial Times calculated that investors sought to get back more than $20B  from private credit firms. Just over 50% of the requests were fulfilled.

The SEC enforcement investigations, which are in early stages, are assessing how credit managers value the loans they hold and whether they're adhering to policies they disclose to investors, the WSJ said. Separate from the enforcement division, SEC examiners have also been asking more questions, it added.

Some people told the WSJ that examiners are engaged in a wide-ranging inspection of Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL). That began when the firm faced opposition to its plan to merge two of its retail-focused loan funds.

Meanwhile, the Treasury Department has sent requests to private credit fund managers and insurance companies after months of individual meetings with fund managers. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has expressed concern about the potential for spillover into the regulated financial system.

The Treasury, the SEC, and the Federal Reserve have focused on how much leverage the private fund managers have, whether the funding provided by banks is irrevocable, and what the result would be if a bank pulled financing, the people told the newspaper.

A wide swath of asset managers and alternative asset managers offer strategies that allow investors to invest in private credit, such as Apollo Global Management (APO),  Ares Management (ARES), Blackstone (BX), KKR (KKR), and BlackRock (BLK). Moreover, business development companies specialize in providing direct lending to middle-market private companies, with a heavy concentration in software. Large BDCs include Prospect Capital (PSEC), Main Street Capital (MAIN), Ares Capital (ARCC), MidCap Financial Investment (MFC), and Hercules Capital (HTGC).

The VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) has dropped 15% in the past year, compared with the S&P 500's 35% increase. The Virtus Private Credit Strategy ETF (VPC) has declined 20% in the past year.

data.title: Blackstone-exposed ETFs ranked by YTD performance after strong Q1 results
data.description:
Blackstone (BX) kicked off 2026 with a solid first quarter, as continued investor inflows and broad market appreciation lifted results across most of its strategies.

Infrastructure and Tactical Opportunities delivered the strongest returns of the period, while Liquid Credit and Opportunistic Real Estate were the lone drags, posting negative returns for the quarter.

Against that backdrop, the following list ranks ETFs with meaningful exposure to Blackstone by their year-to-date performance, offering a lens into how the broader alternative asset manager space has fared through the first four months of 2026.

The performance gap within this group is striking. TBG Dividend Focus ETF (TBG) leads with a YTD gain of 6.68%, followed by The Free Markets ETF (FMKT) at 2.99% and State Street SPDR S&P Capital Markets ETF (KCE) at 2.17%. KCE also stands out as the highest-rated fund in the group on a quant basis, carrying a Buy rating with a score of 3.94, suggesting the broader capital markets ETF may offer a more favorable risk/reward profile than the pure-play alternatives-focused funds.

That contrast is sharp at the bottom of the list. VanEck Alternative Asset Manager ETF (GPZ) and Tema Alternative Asset Managers ETF (AAUM) sit at the bottom with YTD losses of 10.70% and 10.40%, respectively, while WarCap Unconstrained Equity ETF (WCAP) is down 9.51%. Notably, GPZ, AAUM, and WCAP all lack Quant ratings coverage, leaving investors with less quantitative guidance on those names.

Here is the list:

  1. TBG Dividend Focus ETF (TBG), YTD performance: 6.68%

  2. The Free Markets ETF (FMKT), YTD performance: 2.99%

  3. State Street SPDR S&P Capital Markets ETF (KCE), YTD performance: 2.17%, Quant Rating: Buy (3.94)

  4. Invesco S&P 500 Eql Wght Financials ETF (RSPF), YTD performance: -2.17%

  5. Founder-Led ETF (FDRS), YTD performance: -4.54%

  6. Founders 100 ETF (FFF), YTD performance: -4.69%

  7. Invesco Global Listed Private Equity ETF (PSP), YTD performance: -6.58%

  8. WarCap Unconstrained Equity ETF (WCAP), YTD performance: -9.51%

  9. Tema Alternative Asset Managers ETF (AAUM), YTD performance: -10.40%

  10. VanEck Alternative Asset Manager ETF (GPZ), YTD performance: -10.70%

data.title: Comcast anticipates converting the significant majority of free wireless lines to paid in 2H 2026 as Peacock is expected to approach profitability next quarter
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Comcast (CMCSA) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Comcast CEO Brian Roberts said the company is “off to a good start” after “made some real changes,” adding, “with our new leadership structure, Mike as co-CEO and taking the day-to-day lead on improvements and Steve off to a fast start fully running connectivity and platforms, I really like our team.” (Chief Executive Officer, President Brian Roberts)
  • Management framed the quarter as early evidence that the connectivity pivot is gaining traction. Co-CEO Michael Cavanagh said, “broadband net losses improved by more than 100,000 year-over-year,” and “we also delivered the best wireless net additions of any quarter in our history.” (Co-CEO Michael Cavanagh)
  • Cavanagh highlighted media and streaming momentum tied to major sports programming, saying “Legendary February was a remarkable 17-day stretch,” and that NBCU delivered “record advertising sales, roughly $2 billion over the 17 days,” while Peacock “added 2 million net new subscribers…putting Peacock on track to approach profitability for the first time next quarter.” (Co-CEO Cavanagh)
  • CFO Jason Armstrong emphasized the post-Versant reporting reset and the investment posture: “all year-over-year comparisons will be presented on a pro forma basis,” and “this is an investment period for us.” (Chief Financial Officer Jason Armstrong)

Outlook

  • Comcast did not provide consolidated revenue or EPS guidance in the prepared remarks or Q&A.
  • Armstrong reiterated near-term pressure in Connectivity & Platforms profitability: “we were transparent about this last year…including the quarter we’re reporting now and some incremental pressure in the second quarter,” and “that expectation remains unchanged.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)
  • Management pointed to a second-half monetization inflection tied to free wireless lines. Armstrong said, “we anticipate some relief as we exit this year…as we…monetize the free lines at the 1-year anniversary mark,” and later added, “in the second half of the year, many of the free lines will come up for monetization.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)
  • On Peacock, Armstrong said, “we expect…second quarter [to reflect] a meaningful inflection point with Peacock expected to approach profitability.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

Financial Results

  • Comcast reported “revenue increased 11%,” while “excluding [the Olympics and Super Bowl], revenue was up low-single digits,” and “adjusted EBITDA declined 9%.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)
  • Profit and cash generation were described as “earnings per share were $0.79,” and “we generated $3.9 billion of free cash flow…[and] returned $2.5 billion to shareholders, including $1.25 billion in share repurchases.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)
  • In Connectivity & Platforms, Armstrong attributed profitability pressure to pricing simplification and bundling: “simplified pricing and the inclusion of bundled free wireless lines have put pressure on broadband ARPU,” and “weighed on EBITDA growth…evident in our 4.7% decline this quarter.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)
  • In Media, Armstrong said “Media EBITDA was a loss of $426 million,” characterizing Q1 as “peak EBITDA dilution from NBA costs,” while adding, “we feel good about the direction from here.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)

Q&A

  • Craig Moffett, MoffettNathanson: asked how much further broadband ARPU may need to fall and whether stabilization was driven by fixed wireless vs. fiber; Steven Croney replied, “we do see some incremental pressure in Q2, but we do expect relief as we exit the year,” and on competitive sources, “we saw improvement across all of our competitive environments.” (Chief Executive Officer, Connectivity & Platforms Steven Croney)
  • Michael Rollins, Citigroup: asked about wireless success with larger families, business MVNO opportunity, and subsidy dynamics; Croney said, “about 30% of our connects…are coming from existing mobile customers adding more mobile lines,” and on subsidies, “we primarily compete on price and value.” (Chief Executive Officer, Connectivity & Platforms Croney)
  • John Hodulik, UBS: asked if broadband losses can improve for the year and about cable consolidation; Croney said, “we do expect improvement year-over-year,” while Brian Roberts said, “if we can find ways to create shareholder value, the bar is high,” but added, “I also just really do like the direction of the company and don’t want to create a lot of distraction.” (Chief Executive Officer, President Roberts)
  • Jessica Reif Cohen, BofA Securities: asked about capital allocation across NBCU assets and whether Peacock profitability can be consistent; Cavanagh said Peacock should “approach profitability in the second quarter,” and added management has “our sights set on…ongoing and durable profitability for Peacock.” (Co-CEO Cavanagh)
  • Sean Diffley, Morgan Stanley: asked about satellite vs. fixed wireless learnings and potential regulatory implications; Croney said fixed wireless and satellite are “capacity constrained,” and that Comcast’s network “does exceed the capabilities of fixed wireless and satellite.” (Chief Executive Officer, Connectivity & Platforms Croney)
  • Sebastiano Petti, JPMorgan: asked about macro impact on parks and ads plus fiber overlap; Cavanagh said “thus far, not seeing a pullback…that’s concerning,” and Croney answered fiber overlap: “about 55%.” (Co-CEO Cavanagh; Chief Executive Officer, Connectivity & Platforms Croney)
  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs: asked about free-line-to-paid confidence and broadband ARPU effects; Croney said, “in the early cohorts, we’ve seen a significant majority of those customers rolling to paid,” and added, “it will have a direct impact on broadband ARPU…as those lines roll to paid in the back half of the year.” (Chief Executive Officer, Connectivity & Platforms Croney)

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral-to-slightly negative, with repeated probing on broadband ARPU deterioration, sustainability of subscriber improvements, and competitive threats (fixed wireless, fiber, satellite), including questions like “how much lower do you think broadband ARPU might have to go” (Craig Moffett, MoffettNathanson) and whether promotional gains are repeatable (John Hodulik, UBS).
  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly pairing “intense” competition with confidence in execution; Croney said, “we do expect relief as we exit the year,” and Roberts said, “we’ve corrected…way too much negativity.” (Chief Executive Officer, Connectivity & Platforms Croney; Chief Executive Officer, President Roberts)
  • Versus Q4 2025, management tone appeared more execution-and-results oriented, shifting from “inflection point” framing to citing “early signs” and “encouraging” results in broadband and wireless performance. (Co-CEO Cavanagh)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The core connectivity narrative carried over, but Q1 added concrete traction: Q4 emphasized that “broadband subscriber losses were 181,000” and ARPU pressures ahead; Q1 emphasized that “broadband subscriber losses improved…to 65,000” while acknowledging “incremental pressure in Q2.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)
  • Wireless remained positioned as the key convergence lever in both quarters, but Q1 sharpened the monetization timing and product push with Mobile Plus; Cavanagh described it as “a disruptive shift away from the traditional pay-per-device model.” (Co-CEO Cavanagh)
  • Analyst focus stayed anchored on broadband competition and ARPU, but Q1 introduced more direct questioning on satellite’s promotional posture and potential regulatory implications. (Sean Diffley, Morgan Stanley)

Risks and Concerns

  • Management repeatedly described the connectivity market as structurally difficult: “fixed wireless continues to market aggressively,” “fiber overbuild is moving at a rapid pace,” and “we’re not assuming this gets easier anytime soon.” (Co-CEO Cavanagh)
  • Execution risk around pricing reset and ARPU pressure remained explicit: “simplified pricing…free wireless lines…put pressure on broadband ARPU,” with “some incremental pressure in the second quarter.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong)
  • Parks risks were tied to international travel and macro sensitivity: Armstrong cited “impact from China-related inbound travel trends” in Osaka and “a more challenging macroeconomic environment” in Beijing, while Cavanagh said the U.S. parks had not yet shown “a pullback…that’s concerning.” (Chief Financial Officer Armstrong; Co-CEO Cavanagh)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 2026 as an early proof point that Comcast’s connectivity pivot is working, citing improved broadband net losses and record wireless net additions while acknowledging near-term ARPU and EBITDA pressure that is expected to persist into Q2. The company tied a second-half inflection to monetizing free wireless lines, with leadership stating a “significant majority” of early cohorts are rolling to paid and that this should become a tailwind to convergence revenue and broadband ARPU as the year progresses. In Content & Experiences, management highlighted sports-driven scale and advertising, subscriber gains at Peacock, and an expectation that Peacock will “approach profitability” in Q2, while reiterating that Q1 represented the peak dilution from NBA-related costs.
